content.text: The tool inventory is the complete set of tools an agent can call in a given run. It is passed as the
call. Each entry is a dict with three fields: a name the model uses in
blocks, a description the model reads to decide whether to call the tool, and an

that specifies what parameters the model must supply. The quality of tool descriptions has a direct effect on agent behaviour: a vague description causes the model to pick the wrong tool or misuse it; a precise description guides it to the right choice.

attrs.alt: Tool inventory — structure of a tool definition and how the model uses it

content.text: Every tool definition has three required keys. The model reads all three before deciding whether and how to call the tool:
What the model uses it for
Identifier used in tool_use blocks; must be unique in the inventory
Decides whether to call this tool vs. another; reads it before generating arguments
Determines which parameters to supply and what types they expect
description guides selection, not just usage
— the model reads the description to decide which tool fits the current step. Two tools with overlapping descriptions cause the model to pick arbitrarily.
are optional. The model may omit them; the tool implementation must handle their absence.
parameter descriptions are as important as the schema
field tells the model what value to pass. Without it, the model guesses.
Building Tool Definitions as Typed Objects
Raw dicts are error-prone at scale. A dataclass that serialises to the expected dict shape makes it easier to define tools consistently and catch mistakes before they reach the API:
ToolParam carries both schema and documentation
feeds the model. Both fields are required.
— most parameters should be required. Marking a parameter optional means the tool implementation must handle its absence without raising.
to_dict() produces the exact shape expected
can be passed directly into the
An inventory stores both tool definitions and their implementations together, so the dispatch step at tool call time does not need to maintain a separate lookup:
definitions() and dispatch() are the two interfaces
is called in the loop when the model returns a
— if the model calls a tool that is not in the inventory,
with the available names. This surfaces promptly rather than failing silently.
— different tasks can use different inventories (subsets, or different implementations of the same tool). Pass the task-appropriate inventory to the loop.
The description is the model's only guide for deciding when to call a tool. Four pieces of information make a description useful:

# The description is the primary signal the model uses to decide when to call a tool.
# Vague descriptions cause wrong tool selection; good descriptions prevent it.

# POOR — model cannot tell when to prefer this over a web search
BAD_SEARCH_DESC = "Search for information."

# GOOD — scope, data source, and return value are clear
GOOD_SEARCH_DESC = (
    "Search the repository codebase for a string or pattern using ripgrep. "
    "Use this to find function definitions, class names, or any text in source files. "
    "Returns matching lines with file paths and line numbers. "
    "Does NOT search the web or external documentation."
)

# POOR — model does not know this is destructive or when NOT to use it
BAD_DELETE_DESC = "Delete a file."

# GOOD — consequence is stated; model is warned to verify first
GOOD_DELETE_DESC = (
    "Permanently delete a file from the filesystem. "
    "This cannot be undone. "
    "Use read_file to verify the file contents before deleting. "
    "Returns the path that was deleted."
)

# Key principles for descriptions:
# 1. State what the tool does (action + object).
# 2. State when to use it (vs. alternatives).
# 3. State what it returns (so the model knows what to expect).
# 4. State consequences for destructive tools (so the model proceeds carefully).
"Read the full contents of a file"
"Use this before writing to avoid data loss"
Write descriptions for the model, not for the developer. A developer reading the code already knows what read_file does; the model reads the description cold, with no other context.

For agents with large tool inventories, organising by category makes it possible to build task-specific subsets, route different steps to different tool sets, and reason about which capabilities are in scope:

# Organising a large inventory by category helps trace which tools are in use
# and makes it easier to build task-specific subsets.

from enum import Enum


class ToolCategory(Enum):
    FILE_SYSTEM = "file_system"
    CODE_EXECUTION = "code_execution"
    SEARCH = "search"
    NETWORK = "network"
    DATABASE = "database"


@dataclass
class CategorisedToolDef(ToolDef):
    category: ToolCategory = ToolCategory.FILE_SYSTEM


# Build a categorised inventory
ALL_TOOLS: list[CategorisedToolDef] = [
    CategorisedToolDef(
        name="read_file",
        description="Read the full contents of a file.",
        params=[ToolParam("path", "string", "Absolute path to read.")],
        category=ToolCategory.FILE_SYSTEM,
    ),
    CategorisedToolDef(
        name="run_tests",
        description="Run pytest. Call after every code change.",
        params=[ToolParam("path", "string", "Test path.", required=False)],
        category=ToolCategory.CODE_EXECUTION,
    ),
    CategorisedToolDef(
        name="search_code",
        description="Search codebase with ripgrep. Returns file:line matches.",
        params=[
            ToolParam("pattern", "string", "Regex pattern to search for."),
            ToolParam("directory", "string", "Directory to search in.", required=False),
        ],
        category=ToolCategory.SEARCH,
    ),
]


def tools_for_category(category: ToolCategory) -> list[dict]:
    """Return only the tools in a given category, as API dicts."""
    return [t.to_dict() for t in ALL_TOOLS if t.category == category]


def tools_for_task(task: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Select a minimal subset of tools based on what the task requires."""
    needed: set[ToolCategory] = set()
    lower = task.lower()
    if any(w in lower for w in ("file", "read", "write", "modify")):
        needed.add(ToolCategory.FILE_SYSTEM)
    if any(w in lower for w in ("test", "run", "execute")):
        needed.add(ToolCategory.CODE_EXECUTION)
    if any(w in lower for w in ("find", "search", "locate", "grep")):
        needed.add(ToolCategory.SEARCH)
    return [t.to_dict() for t in ALL_TOOLS if t.category in needed]
— passing only the tools a task needs reduces the model's decision surface. Fewer choices → fewer wrong selections.
selects based on keywords in the task description.
enum makes it easy to audit what an agent can do: file system, network, database. Useful for security review and constraint checking.
) so tool names within a category sort and read together.

import pytest


class TestToolDef:
    def test_to_dict_shape(self):
        tool = ToolDef(
            name="read_file",
            description="Read a file.",
            params=[ToolParam("path", "string", "Path to read.")],
        )
        d = tool.to_dict()
        assert d["name"] == "read_file"
        assert d["input_schema"]["type"] == "object"
        assert "path" in d["input_schema"]["properties"]
        assert "path" in d["input_schema"]["required"]

    def test_optional_param_not_in_required(self):
        tool = ToolDef(
            name="run_tests",
            description="Run tests.",
            params=[ToolParam("path", "string", "Test path.", required=False)],
        )
        d = tool.to_dict()
        assert "path" not in d["input_schema"]["required"]
        assert "path" in d["input_schema"]["properties"]


class TestToolInventory:
    def _make_inventory(self):
        inv = ToolInventory()
        inv.register(
            ToolDef("echo", "Echo input.", [ToolParam("msg", "string", "Message.")]),
            lambda msg: msg,
        )
        return inv

    def test_definitions_returns_list(self):
        inv = self._make_inventory()
        defs = inv.definitions()
        assert isinstance(defs, list)
        assert defs[0]["name"] == "echo"

    def test_dispatch_calls_implementation(self):
        inv = self._make_inventory()
        result = inv.dispatch("echo", {"msg": "hello"})
        assert result == "hello"

    def test_dispatch_unknown_tool_raises(self):
        inv = self._make_inventory()
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Unknown tool"):
            inv.dispatch("nonexistent", {})
The tool inventory is passed as

(specifies parameters). Write descriptions for the model: state the action, when to use it, what it returns, and consequences for destructive tools. Build definitions as typed objects (
) to catch errors before they reach the API. Store definitions and implementations together in an inventory so dispatch is a single
call. For large inventories, organise by category and build task-specific subsets — fewer tools means fewer wrong selections.
